questions.question: What is the effect size (g) for CBT-based interventions targeting maladaptive social cognition in loneliness reduction?

options.text: g = -0.10 (very small effect)
g = -0.25 (small effect)
g = -0.46 (moderate effect)
g = -0.80 (large effect)

questions.explanation: The 2025 meta-analysis (Birken et al.) found CBT targeting maladaptive social cognition produced g = -0.463 — a moderate effect size and the STRONGEST intervention mechanism for loneliness reduction. This outperforms social skills training alone, increasing social opportunities alone, or enhancing social support alone. The key insight: changing how you think about social situations is more powerful than simply having more social contact.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the four maintaining cognitive patterns in loneliness?

options.text: Hypervigilance to social threat
Negative prediction (anticipatory rejection)
Lack of social skills or conversational ability
Self-focused attention during interaction

questions.explanation: The four maintaining patterns are: (1) hypervigilance to threat, (2) negative prediction, (3) self-focused attention, and (4) post-event rumination. 'Lack of social skills' is NOT listed because research shows maladaptive COGNITION (how you interpret social situations) is the primary maintaining factor — not skill deficits. You can have excellent social skills but still feel lonely if your brain is telling you 'people don't like me.'

questions.question: What is the purpose of generating alternative explanations in attribution flexibility practice?

options.text: To deny that rejection ever happens
To prove that your initial negative thought was definitely wrong
To recognize that ambiguous social signals have multiple plausible interpretations, not just the most negative one
To convince yourself that everyone likes you

questions.explanation: Attribution flexibility is about correcting for the negativity bias amplified by loneliness. Most social signals are ambiguous (delayed text, brief conversation, declined invitation). Rejection-sensitive individuals automatically jump to the most negative interpretation. Generating 3-5 alternative explanations helps you recognize: 'This is ONE possibility, not the only one.' You're not denying rejection — you're correcting for interpretive bias.

questions.question: What is the Clark & Wells attention training technique for social anxiety?

options.text: Focusing intensely on your own performance during conversations to improve it
Deliberately shifting attention from self-focused monitoring to external focus on the other person
Avoiding eye contact to reduce anxiety
Rehearsing what you'll say next while the other person is talking

questions.explanation: The Clark & Wells protocol (highest-efficacy treatment for SAD) includes attention training: shifting from self-focused monitoring ('Am I sounding stupid? Do I look weird?') to external focus on the other person (their words, expressions, interests). Self-focused attention prevents genuine connection and amplifies anxiety. External focus improves both connection quality and reduces anxiety.

questions.question: When should you use behavioral experiments to test negative social predictions?

options.text: Never — cognitive reframing alone is sufficient
Only when you're feeling completely confident
After cognitive restructuring, to gather real data about whether your predictions are accurate
Only if you have clinical Social Anxiety Disorder

questions.explanation: Behavioral experiments are essential for testing predictions. Thought records challenge negative thoughts intellectually, but behavioral experiments provide REAL DATA. Most negative social predictions are overstated ('80% of people will reject me' → actual data shows 0% hostile, 40% neutral, 60% welcoming). Testing predictions breaks the avoidance cycle and builds disconfirming evidence. Start with low-stakes experiments while anxious — confidence follows action.

title: The Loneliness Thought Loop — CBT Cognitive Reframing - Quiz
